{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01EFE6oktNEAoRy7bKnbKkik","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Perfect! I found a reference to `avoid-prototype-pollution` in the eslint config. This is a key indicator. Now let me compile all the files I've found that have prototype pollution defenses. This pattern appears across buffer handling, cluster management, process spawning, error handling, and various internal utilities like validators and crypto operations.\n\n`SafeMap` and `SafeSet` implementations are utilized throughout the codebase for secure data structure operations. comprehensive prototype pollution defenses through multiple strategies:\\n\\n1. **Object.create(null) and __proto__: null**: The codebase extensively uses these patterns to create objects without prototype chains. This is the foundational defense against prototype pollution, as demonstrated in files like lib/_http_agent.js (line 96, 106-108), lib/vm.js (line 397), lib/internal/util.js (line 733), lib/internal/timers.js (line 157, 162), and lib/internal/fs/glob.js (lines 103-104, 144). The comment in lib/internal/http2/compat.js (lines 317-319) explicitly notes that Object.create(null) is intentional for HTTP/2 headers initialization.\\n\\n2. **Safe Property Access with hasOwnProperty Checks**: Core modules use ObjectPrototypeHasOwnProperty (imported from primordials) instead of direct property access. This is seen in lib/internal/util.js (line 739), lib/buffer.js (line 977), lib/internal/validators.js (line 538), and lib/internal/crypto/util.js (lines 548-549). This pattern prevents prototype chain traversal attacks.\\n\\n3. **Primordials System**: The lib/internal/per_context/primordials.js file implements safe wrappers for built-in objects and iterators (SafeMap, SafeSet, SafeWeakMap, SafeWeakRef, SafeArrayIterator, etc.). These are used throughout the codebase to prevent tampering with built-in prototypes and provide secure iteration mechanisms. Key files importing from primordials include lib/internal/modules/esm/translators.js, lib/internal/perf/observe.js, lib/internal/console/constructor.js, and lib/internal/modules/helpers.js.\\n\\n4. **Safe Merge and Assign Patterns**: The filterOwnProperties() function in lib/internal/util.js (lines 735-745) demonstrates the safe merge pattern - it uses Object.create(null) for the target object and explicitly checks hasOwnProperty before copying properties. This pattern is used in lib/internal/util.js (lines 738-741) and similar safe assignment patterns appear in lib/internal/webidl.js (line 300).\\n\\n5. **ObjectFreeze Usage**: Core modules use ObjectFreeze on configuration objects to prevent modification. Examples include lib/constants.js (line 40-41), lib/vm.js (line 402), lib/tls.js (lines 117, 131), lib/async_hooks.js (line 293), lib/internal/modules/helpers.js (lines 92-93, 486-488), and lib/internal/modules/cjs/loader.js (line 471). This is particularly important for preventing prototype pollution through property descriptor manipulation.\\n\\n6. **Type Checking with ObjectPrototypeIsPrototypeOf**: Files like lib/assert.js (lines 524, 689), lib/internal/webidl.js (line 377), and lib/internal/crypto/webidl.js (line 328) use ObjectPrototypeIsPrototypeOf for safe type checking instead of instanceof, which can be spoofed through prototype manipulation.\\n\\n7. **ESLint Enforcement**: The lib/eslint.config_partial.mjs file (line 411) enforces the 'node-core/avoid-prototype-pollution' ESLint rule, indicating that prototype pollution prevention is a critical security requirement enforced at the linting level.\\n\\nThese defenses work together to create a multi-layered protection strategy where: (1) objects are created without prototypes, (2) property access is carefully validated, (3) built-in objects are wrapped with safe versions, (4) critical objects are frozen, and (5) the code is linted to catch prototype pollution vulnerabilities. This comprehensive approach ensures that core Node.js modules are resistant to prototype pollution attacks even when processing untrusted data from user inputs, network requests, or third-party code.\"\n}\n"},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":12,"cache_creation_input_tokens":1851,"cache_read_input_tokens":65944,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":1851},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"27fec8e0-f5bc-4cd2-89cc-559105457f1f","uuid":"70251939-d5a5-466e-b896-7387cd044d49"}

comprehensive prototype pollution defenses through multiple strategies:\\n\\n1. **Object.create(null) and __proto__: null**: The codebase extensively uses these patterns to create objects without prototype chains. This is the foundational defense against prototype pollution, as demonstrated in files like lib/_http_agent.js (line 96, 106-108), lib/vm.js (line 397), lib/internal/util.js (line 733), lib/internal/timers.js (line 157, 162), and lib/internal/fs/glob.js (lines 103-104, 144). The comment in lib/internal/http2/compat.js (lines 317-319) explicitly notes that Object.create(null) is intentional for HTTP/2 headers initialization.\\n\\n2. **Safe Property Access with hasOwnProperty Checks**: Core modules use ObjectPrototypeHasOwnProperty (imported from primordials) instead of direct property access. This is seen in lib/internal/util.js (line 739), lib/buffer.js (line 977), lib/internal/validators.js (line 538), and lib/internal/crypto/util.js (lines 548-549). This pattern prevents prototype chain traversal attacks.\\n\\n3. **Primordials System**: The lib/internal/per_context/primordials.js file implements safe wrappers for built-in objects and iterators (SafeMap, SafeSet, SafeWeakMap, SafeWeakRef, SafeArrayIterator, etc.). These are used throughout the codebase to prevent tampering with built-in prototypes and provide secure iteration mechanisms. Key files importing from primordials include lib/internal/modules/esm/translators.js, lib/internal/perf/observe.js, lib/internal/console/constructor.js, and lib/internal/modules/helpers.js.\\n\\n4. **Safe Merge and Assign Patterns**: The filterOwnProperties() function in lib/internal/util.js (lines 735-745) demonstrates the safe merge pattern - it uses Object.create(null) for the target object and explicitly checks hasOwnProperty before copying properties. This pattern is used in lib/internal/util.js (lines 738-741) and similar safe assignment patterns appear in lib/internal/webidl.js (line 300).\\n\\n5. **ObjectFreeze Usage**: Core modules use ObjectFreeze on configuration objects to prevent modification. Examples include lib/constants.js (line 40-41), lib/vm.js (line 402), lib/tls.js (lines 117, 131), lib/async_hooks.js (line 293), lib/internal/modules/helpers.js (lines 92-93, 486-488), and lib/internal/modules/cjs/loader.js (line 471). This is particularly important for preventing prototype pollution through property descriptor manipulation.\\n\\n6. **Type Checking with ObjectPrototypeIsPrototypeOf**: Files like lib/assert.js (lines 524, 689), lib/internal/webidl.js (line 377), and lib/internal/crypto/webidl.js (line 328) use ObjectPrototypeIsPrototypeOf for safe type checking instead of instanceof, which can be spoofed through prototype manipulation.\\n\\n7. **ESLint Enforcement**: The lib/eslint.config_partial.mjs file (line 411) enforces the 'node-core/avoid-prototype-pollution' ESLint rule, indicating that prototype pollution prevention is a critical security requirement enforced at the linting level.\\n\\nThese defenses work together to create a multi-layered protection strategy where: (1) objects are created without prototypes, (2) property access is carefully validated, (3) built-in objects are wrapped with safe versions, (4) critical objects are frozen, and (5) the code is linted to catch prototype pollution vulnerabilities. This comprehensive approach ensures that core Node.js modules are resistant to prototype pollution attacks even when processing untrusted data from user inputs, network requests, or third-party code.\"\n}\n","structuredPatch":[],"originalFile":null}}

\"lib/internal/per_context/primordials.js\", \"symbol\": \"makeSafe\"}\n    77→  ],\n    78→  \"text\": \"Node.js core implements comprehensive prototype pollution defenses through multiple strategies:\\n\\n1. **Object.create(null) and __proto__: null**: The codebase extensively uses these patterns to create objects without prototype chains. This is the foundational defense against prototype pollution, as demonstrated in files like lib/_http_agent.js (line 96, 106-108), lib/vm.js (line 397), lib/internal/util.js (line 733), lib/internal/timers.js (line 157, 162), and lib/internal/fs/glob.js (lines 103-104, 144). The comment in lib/internal/http2/compat.js (lines 317-319) explicitly notes that Object.create(null) is intentional for HTTP/2 headers initialization.\\n\\n2. **Safe Property Access with hasOwnProperty Checks**: Core modules use ObjectPrototypeHasOwnProperty (imported from primordials) instead of direct property access. This is seen in lib/internal/util.js (line 739), lib/buffer.js (line 977), lib/internal/validators.js (line 538), and lib/internal/crypto/util.js (lines 548-549). This pattern prevents prototype chain traversal attacks.\\n\\n3. **Primordials System**: The lib/internal/per_context/primordials.js file implements safe wrappers for built-in objects and iterators (SafeMap, SafeSet, SafeWeakMap, SafeWeakRef, SafeArrayIterator, etc.). These are used throughout the codebase to prevent tampering with built-in prototypes and provide secure iteration mechanisms. Key files importing from primordials include lib/internal/modules/esm/translators.js, lib/internal/perf/observe.js, lib/internal/console/constructor.js, and lib/internal/modules/helpers.js.\\n\\n4. **Safe Merge and Assign Patterns**: The filterOwnProperties() function in lib/internal/util.js (lines 735-745) demonstrates the safe merge pattern - it uses Object.create(null) for the target object and explicitly checks hasOwnProperty before copying properties. This pattern is used in lib/internal/util.js (lines 738-741) and similar safe assignment patterns appear in lib/internal/webidl.js (line 300).\\n\\n5. **ObjectFreeze Usage**: Core modules use ObjectFreeze on configuration objects to prevent modification. Examples include lib/constants.js (line 40-41), lib/vm.js (line 402), lib/tls.js (lines 117, 131), lib/async_hooks.js (line 293), lib/internal/modules/helpers.js (lines 92-93, 486-488), and lib/internal/modules/cjs/loader.js (line 471). This is particularly important for preventing prototype pollution through property descriptor manipulation.\\n\\n6. **Type Checking with ObjectPrototypeIsPrototypeOf**: Files like lib/assert.js (lines 524, 689), lib/internal/webidl.js (line 377), and lib/internal/crypto/webidl.js (line 328) use ObjectPrototypeIsPrototypeOf for safe type checking instead of instanceof, which can be spoofed through prototype manipulation.\\n\\n7. **ESLint Enforcement**: The lib/eslint.config_partial.mjs file (line 411) enforces the 'node-core/avoid-prototype-pollution' ESLint rule, indicating that prototype pollution prevention is a critical security requirement enforced at the linting level.\\n\\nThese defenses work together to create a multi-layered protection strategy where: (1) objects are created without prototypes, (2) property access is carefully validated, (3) built-in objects are wrapped with safe versions, (4) critical objects are frozen, and (5) the code is linted to catch prototype pollution vulnerabilities. This comprehensive approach ensures that core Node.js modules are resistant to prototype pollution attacks even when processing untrusted data from user inputs, network requests, or third-party code.\"\n    79→}\n    80→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. comprehensive prototype pollution defenses through multiple strategies:\\n\\n1. **Object.create(null) and __proto__: null**: The codebase extensively uses these patterns to create objects without prototype chains. This is the foundational defense against prototype pollution, as demonstrated in files like lib/_http_agent.js (line 96, 106-108), lib/vm.js (line 397), lib/internal/util.js (line 733), lib/internal/timers.js (line 157, 162), and lib/internal/fs/glob.js (lines 103-104, 144). The comment in lib/internal/http2/compat.js (lines 317-319) explicitly notes that Object.create(null) is intentional for HTTP/2 headers initialization.\\n\\n2. **Safe Property Access with hasOwnProperty Checks**: Core modules use ObjectPrototypeHasOwnProperty (imported from primordials) instead of direct property access. This is seen in lib/internal/util.js (line 739), lib/buffer.js (line 977), lib/internal/validators.js (line 538), and lib/internal/crypto/util.js (lines 548-549). This pattern prevents prototype chain traversal attacks.\\n\\n3. **Primordials System**: The lib/internal/per_context/primordials.js file implements safe wrappers for built-in objects and iterators (SafeMap, SafeSet, SafeWeakMap, SafeWeakRef, SafeArrayIterator, etc.). These are used throughout the codebase to prevent tampering with built-in prototypes and provide secure iteration mechanisms. Key files importing from primordials include lib/internal/modules/esm/translators.js, lib/internal/perf/observe.js, lib/internal/console/constructor.js, and lib/internal/modules/helpers.js.\\n\\n4. **Safe Merge and Assign Patterns**: The filterOwnProperties() function in lib/internal/util.js (lines 735-745) demonstrates the safe merge pattern - it uses Object.create(null) for the target object and explicitly checks hasOwnProperty before copying properties. This pattern is used in lib/internal/util.js (lines 738-741) and similar safe assignment patterns appear in lib/internal/webidl.js (line 300).\\n\\n5. **ObjectFreeze Usage**: Core modules use ObjectFreeze on configuration objects to prevent modification. Examples include lib/constants.js (line 40-41), lib/vm.js (line 402), lib/tls.js (lines 117, 131), lib/async_hooks.js (line 293), lib/internal/modules/helpers.js (lines 92-93, 486-488), and lib/internal/modules/cjs/loader.js (line 471). This is particularly important for preventing prototype pollution through property descriptor manipulation.\\n\\n6. **Type Checking with ObjectPrototypeIsPrototypeOf**: Files like lib/assert.js (lines 524, 689), lib/internal/webidl.js (line 377), and lib/internal/crypto/webidl.js (line 328) use ObjectPrototypeIsPrototypeOf for safe type checking instead of instanceof, which can be spoofed through prototype manipulation.\\n\\n7. **ESLint Enforcement**: The lib/eslint.config_partial.mjs file (line 411) enforces the 'node-core/avoid-prototype-pollution' ESLint rule, indicating that prototype pollution prevention is a critical security requirement enforced at the linting level.\\n\\nThese defenses work together to create a multi-layered protection strategy where: (1) objects are created without prototypes, (2) property access is carefully validated, (3) built-in objects are wrapped with safe versions, (4) critical objects are frozen, and (5) the code is linted to catch prototype pollution vulnerabilities. This comprehensive approach ensures that core Node.js modules are resistant to prototype pollution attacks even when processing untrusted data from user inputs, network requests, or third-party code.\"\n}\n","numLines":80,"startLine":1,"totalLines":80}}}
